inbound.subject: Starting over after a layoff
inbound.body: Hi coach, I want to get back to running after a messy few months. My big goal is to get healthy enough to race a half marathon again this spring. Right now I mostly need help rebuilding without doing something stupid.

outbound.subject: Re: Starting over after a layoff
outbound.text:
Thanks — welcome back. I hear you: your goal is to rebuild safely and avoid doing anything "stupid" while getting healthy enough to race a half this spring.
You’ve said the priority is careful rebuilding after a messy few months. That’s exactly where I’d keep us for now: protect tissue, restore repeatability, and only add stress once we see consistent, pain-free sessions.
Before I write a detailed block I need a few quick details so I can tailor progression safely. Please answer these when you reply:
1) Weekly availability: how many days can you realistically run each week, and what’s your longest single training slot (how much continuous time you can commit)?
2) Current injury status / pain: what hurts (where), when it started, what makes it better/worse, and have you seen any clinician or been given advice? 
3) Recent training: last 4 weeks — roughly how many runs or total miles per week, and how long has your layoff been? 
4) Other constraints: work/travel, sleep, major stressors, or anything that will affect consistency. 
5) Target race date (approximate) if you have one.
While you reply, here’s a very conservative 2-week starter you can begin immediately (this is the first fortnight of your return_safely block):
- 3 easy run days: start 20–30 minutes easy; over two weeks build toward 35–40 minutes only if every session is pain-free and feels easy. 
- 1 easy cross-training session: 30–45 minutes cycling, swim, elliptical or a brisk walk. 
- 1 short strength session: 20–30 minutes (bodyweight + core). Doing this twice in the two-week window is fine. 
- 2 full rest days.
- If any session causes discomfort, switch to walk–run intervals (example: run 4–8 minutes, walk 1–2 minutes) or stop the run and replace with cross-training. Keep all runs conversational/easy — no hard/tempo/threshold work yet.
Why this first step: we’re protecting healing tissue and rebuilding the habit of consistent, repeatable sessions. We progress one lever at a time: first frequency/duration, then weekly volume, and only later intensity. I’ll only add harder sessions after multiple stable, pain-free days and consistent completion of the easy sessions.
Coaching cadence and what I need from you:
- Once you confirm availability and injury status I’ll send a tailored weekly plan. 
- After each week reply with: which sessions you completed, any pain (during, after, next morning), sleep quality, stress, and how the easy runs felt (effort and breathing). 
- If pain increases, a new sharp pain appears, or something feels wrong, stop the session and tell me immediately so we reassess.
If it’s OK with you, once you reply I’ll finalize a longer 12-week progressive block aimed at your half. Timeline: after I get your answers I’ll send a personalized week within 48 hours.
Say when you can start and answer the five questions above and I’ll finalize next steps.

inbound.subject: A little more context
inbound.body: A bit more context: I used to run consistently before the layoff and I'd call myself intermediate. I can train four days a week most weeks if we keep it realistic. The thing that nags me is mild Achilles tightness when I get greedy. I also do better when the plan feels structured.

outbound.subject: Re: A little more context
outbound.text:
Great context — thanks for the extra details. You’re middleweight intermediate, want a realistic, structured plan, and you’re aiming to race a half marathon this spring. We’ll honor that with a cautious, risk-managed return-to-running plan that prioritizes consistency and symptom control.
This is the first full plan after intake, shaped to your goal and constraints. We’re in Week 1 of Return-to-Training, focusing on ReturnSafely. The current weekly skeleton is three anchor sessions: easy_aerobic, strength, and tempo, with safety notes that no hard sessions while risk is elevated. You mentioned you can usually train four days a week; I’ll preserve structure with three core sessions and an option to add a fourth easy day or light cross-training if you feel good and pain remains absent. We’ll monitor Achilles tightness carefully and avoid progression until symptoms stay pain-free for a couple of weeks. Please confirm your typical availability so I can lock in a concrete 4th day if you want it. Finally, expect a weekly cadence where we check pain, sleep, and readiness, and adjust only if needed to stay safe.
What I’m asking from you:
- Confirm your four-day availability to lock in a potential extra easy day, or confirm three days if you’d prefer to keep it lean.
- Share any current niggles or pain updates, and any clinicians’ guidance you’re following.
- Share preferred training times or windows that work consistently with your week.
- Any other constraints I should know about.
Cadence and contingencies:
- I’ll check in weekly on pain, sleep, and training response, and keep adjustments conservative and data-driven.
- If pain spikes or symptoms worsen, we drop intensity immediately and revert to the easy anchor sessions only.
- If the week destabilizes, we’ll keep to the easiest anchor sessions until things settle.
You’re in Week 1 of Return-to-Training, Block: return_safely, focusing on consistency and safe loading as we rebuild running mechanics and strength with the Achilles in mind. That’s the frame I’ll use to guide our progress.
Please reply with your available days/times, current pain status, and any clinicians’ guidance you’re following. I’ll use your input to lock in the 4th day if you want it.
Coach

inbound.subject: Profile details and target race
inbound.body: Here is the missing context more directly. Primary goal: run a half marathon on 2026-05-17 and finish feeling strong. Time availability: 4 sessions per week and about 4 to 5 hours total. Experience level: intermediate. Constraints: mild Achilles tightness, busy work mornings on Tuesdays, and I prefer structured guidance.

outbound.subject: Re: Profile details and target race
outbound.text:
Thanks — I’ve got the extra context. That helps a lot. I’ll keep the plan tightly conservative and structured so we protect your Achilles while building toward the half on 2026-05-17.
You’re targeting the half on 2026-05-17 and we’re 18 weeks out. You’re in week 2 of the return_safely block. Given your intermediate background, 4 sessions/week availability (about 4–5 hours), mild Achilles tightness and busy Tuesday mornings, I’m keeping the plan deliberately low-risk and structured so you can rebuild consistency without flares.
Weekly skeleton (minimum-effective-dose) — tentative days (please confirm):
- Monday — easy aerobic: 20–35 minutes easy running (easy breathing, conversational pace).
- Tuesday — short strength: 20–30 minutes (evening or swapped to another day if mornings are busy; keep load low and pain-free).
- Thursday — mobility: 10–15 minutes focused on ankle/calf mobility, hip activation, and posture.
- Saturday — longer easy aerobic: 60–80 minutes easy run (steady, conservative).
Today’s instruction: completely conservative — choose the low end of the ranges, no hard efforts, and pick a session you can repeat tomorrow or the next day. The goal for now is repeatable completion, not intensity.
Why this approach: your Achilles puts you in a yellow-risk zone and we’re early in a return-to-training block. Energy has been the limiting signal, so we prioritize consistency via a single-lever progression (increase duration before intensity) and the minimum effective dose. That reduces flare risk and keeps the plan sustainable.
Safety rules & if/then behaviours:
- If Achilles pain increases during a session, stop or shorten immediately and report the change.
- No hard or threshold sessions until you’ve had multiple consecutive pain-free days.
- If energy drops during a session, cut it short rather than forcing completion.
- If you bounce back the next day, resume cautiously; do not add extra volume to compensate for a lighter day.
- If you’ve had clinician guidance, send it — I won’t replace medical advice but I will integrate their recommendations into training.
What I need from you to finalize the week:
- Preferred time windows for training and your longest single training slot (so I can place the longer run where it fits).
- Confirmation if you want the strength session moved off Tuesday morning (evening or another day).
- Recent running: average weekly mileage for the last 4 weeks.
- Brief Achilles history: when it started, what makes it worse, what eases it, and any clinician input or treatments underway.
Next steps and coaching cadence: once you confirm times and the Achilles details I’ll send the finalized plan for this week. After that you’ll get weekly updates. For day-to-day check-ins, reply with a quick daily note: pain 0–10, sleep (hours/quality), and energy 1–10 — or flag any new or worsening pain immediately.
Reply with your preferred days/times, the 4-week mileage average, and the short Achilles history and I’ll lock this week in.

inbound.subject: Availability update for this week
inbound.body: Availability update for this week. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate feels green. I can run Monday, Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday. Week is not chaotic. Schedule variability is medium because work may move one session by a few hours.

outbound.subject: Re: Availability update for this week
outbound.text:
Availability update received. Pain is low (2/10) and you’ve got four training days. I’ve updated the plan to a four-session base that fits your schedule while keeping your Achilles safe and aligned with your half-marathon goal.
Updated plan: four-session week (Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun):
- Monday — easy_aerobic 20–35 min (plus light mobility warm-up).
- Wednesday — short strength block 15–25 min (bodyweight, emphasis on control and posture).
- Friday — tempo at an easy pace 10–15 min (with full warm-up/cool-down; monitor pain and treat as easy if needed).
- Sunday — long easy run 40–60 min (maintain easy cadence and relaxed form).
Keep load conservative and predictable; downgrade any session immediately if pain or fatigue rises. If a session is moved a few hours or shifted due to work, keep the order and aim to complete all four sessions in the week. Maintain sleep and simple recovery habits to support adaptation. If Friday tempo or Sunday long feels uncomfortable, revert to an easy aerobic equivalent and communicate adjustments early.
This aligns with your current skeleton: easy_aerobic, tempo, strength; base-building focus. Safety note: No hard sessions when risk is red-tier.
Quick confirmations:
- Do you want to lock in specific time windows for Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun?
- What is the longest single training slot you can accommodate this week?
- Any constraints about where to place the strength day?
You’re in Week 3 of the return-safely block, heading toward your May 17 half marathon with 18 weeks to go. We’ll adjust as needed based on how you feel and what the week throws at you.
Awaiting your quick confirmation.

inbound.body: Training check-in. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate is green. I did a run for 45m and 6 km yesterday. Felt good, energy ok, little sore, slept well. Missed sessions count: 0. I want to keep building carefully.

outbound.subject: Re: First check-in after an easy run
outbound.text:
Nice work on the 45-minute easy effort yesterday. It’s a good signal that you can continue with a careful progression while keeping pain very low.
You’re in Week 3 of the return safely block, building toward the May event on 2026-05-17. Current check-in shows pain at 2/10 and risk is green, so we keep the week conservative and focused on safe progression.
Four-session base week (Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun):
- Monday: easy aerobic + mobility (roughly 20–35 minutes).
- Wednesday: short, controlled strength (15–25 minutes, emphasis on posture and movement quality).
- Friday: tempo work kept light (10–15 minutes total); treat as easy if you feel any strain—the goal is controlled load, not intensity.
- Sunday: long easy run (40–60 minutes) to build endurance without provoking the Achilles.
Key guardrails:
- Monitor pain daily; if pain or fatigue rises meaningfully (>3/10) or there’s a sharp change, downgrade that session to easy and don’t try to make up missed intensity later in the week.
- Maintain consistent sleep, hydration, and simple recovery habits.
- If you need to move a session by a few hours, that’s fine—keep the sequence intact.
- Keep cadence light and posture tall during each session.
Recovery guidance:
- Prioritize sleep and steady hydration; even small extra minutes of mobility and light cooldowns help q week.
Please confirm your preferred time slots for Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun, or share any tweaks or constraints for this week. If anything feels off, we can adjust the plan quickly without changing the overall sequence.

inbound.subject: How easy should easy feel?
inbound.body: Quick question: on these comeback runs, how easy should easy actually feel? I'm trying not to chase pace, but I also don't want to jog so slowly that the mechanics get weird.

outbound.subject: Re: How easy should easy feel?
outbound.text:
Nice question — here’s a concise rule-of-thumb for easy comeback runs with a mild Achilles niggle.
During this return safely block, easy should stay truly easy. Use a conversational pace as your benchmark: aim for an RPE around 3–4 out of 10, or roughly 60–70% of heart-rate reserve. If you prefer pace anchors, you should be able to chat in full sentences without gasping. The goal is to rebuild rhythm and mechanics without provoking the niggle, not to chase pace.
Concrete cues to monitor:
- Keep the effort easy on the easy aerobic sessions.
- Listen for the Achilles—if pain or tightness creeps up to around 3/10 or higher, dial back volume or pace that day.
- Hold the mechanics light and relaxed (shorter stride, softer landing) rather than forcing cadence or speed.
For this week, stick to the four-session rhythm (Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun) and avoid hard efforts; use mobility/strength on other days to support Achilles health. If pain worsens or sticks into the next day, reduce volume and defer progression until symptom stability improves.
I can lock in exact times once you share your available windows.
Next step: please reply with your available time windows for Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun, and the longest single training slot you can commit to this week. Once you share, I’ll lock in the schedule.

inbound.subject: Work got messy
inbound.body: Can we adjust the week? Work is crazy and I probably only have Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday for training. I missed one session already and my stress is definitely up.

outbound.subject: Re: Work got messy
outbound.text:
Thanks — I hear you: work is messy this week, you only have Wed/Fri/Sun, you already missed a session and stress is up.
Short plan: I’ll shift this week to a 3-session layout (Wed, Fri, Sun) and keep everything conservative. Priority is completion and recovery over volume or intensity — protect the Achilles and your energy first.
Quick reflection of what I heard
- You have training windows Wed/Fri/Sun, one session is already missed, and work stress is higher this week. I’m keeping the week low-stress and flexible.
Updated weekly skeleton (conservative)
- Wed — easy aerobic: 30–45 minutes, conversational pace (RPE 3–4). If you’re short on time, do 20–30 min and call it done.
- Fri — short strength/skills + mobility: 20–30 minutes (bodyweight or light circuit) plus optional 10–15 min easy jog or walk. Can be done at home or gym depending on you.
- Sun — longer easy aerobic: 60–75 minutes but back off if Achilles or energy worsen. Keep it conversational; no pace targets, just time and feel.
Specific safety rules (non-negotiable)
- Do not try to make up the missed session later in the week. No compensating volume.
- No hard efforts this week — this block is conservative and red-lines on intensity. Treat sessions as maintenance/stress management.
- If energy drops during a session, cut it short rather than pushing. If Achilles pain reaches ~3/10 during or after a session, stop or significantly reduce and report it to me.
Practical time-pressure options
- Shorter run option: 20–30 min easy instead of 45 if your slot is tight.
- No-equipment strength: 2–3 rounds of squats, single-leg step-downs, planks, and glute bridges (30–40 sec work, 20–30 sec rest).
- If Friday won’t work for strength, we can move that block to Wed — tell me which you prefer.
Recovery and stress guidance

inbound.subject: Still on the same four-day pattern
inbound.body: Just to confirm, I'm still doing four days per week most weeks. The same general Monday Wednesday Friday Sunday pattern still works, although Friday has become the easiest day for me to protect.

outbound.subject: Re: Still on the same four-day pattern
outbound.text:
Nice work staying the course with four days a week and recognizing Friday as the easiest day to protect. Here’s a concise, conservative plan update that matches your energy signals and Achilles-safe approach.
You're in week 8 of the return-safely base block, with 13 weeks to your goal event on 2026-05-17. The focus remains conservative: four days per week at the minimum effective dose to protect consistency and recovery, with easy aerobic work, simple strength/technique, and light mobility.
Four-day plan (your current skeleton):
- Monday: easy_aerobic, 25–40 minutes depending on energy
- Wednesday: strength (Achilles-friendly drills + mobility), 25–35 minutes
- Friday: easy_aerobic or easy mobility-only option, 20–30 minutes
- Sunday: skills (technique + mobility), 20–30 minutes
Key rules:
- Stay in the easy zone (RPE 3–4)
- Sleep and nutrition matter; keep stress management in check
- Avoid high-impact or hard efforts today/tomorrow if pain or fatigue rises beyond roughly 2–3/10
If energy drops, cut session short rather than forcing completion. If energy rebounds, resume with caution rather than rushing to catch up.
Your race target remains 2026-05-17. This plan is designed to keep you healthy enough to race while laying a solid foundation.
We’ll stay nimble this week and adjust only if energy signals dictate.

inbound.subject: Travel week coming up
inbound.body: Travel week heads-up: next week is rough. I only have Tuesday, Thursday, and Saturday open, and hotel treadmill plus a tiny gym are all I'll have. I'd love the simplest travel-friendly version of the plan.

outbound.subject: Re: Travel week coming up
outbound.text:
Thanks — good heads-up. I built a 3-session, travel-friendly week that protects the Achilles and keeps the minimum effective dose while you're on the treadmill and a tiny gym.
This is a one-week tweak during week 8 of your return-safely block to preserve the progress toward your half on 2026-05-17 (12 weeks out): keep load low, protect the tendon, and keep consistency rather than chasing volume or intensity.
Plan (Tue / Thu / Sat)
- Tue: Strength — 25–35 min (gym-friendly)
- Thu: Easy aerobic treadmill — 25–40 min (RPE 3–4)
- Sat: Skills/form + short easy run or cross-train — 20–35 min total
Strength session (Tue) — conservative, minimal equipment
- Exercises (2–3 sets each): single-leg RDL (light), step-ups, glute bridge/hip-thrust (bodyweight or light load), band lateral walks, plank variation.
- Reps/time: 8–12 reps or 45–60s per exercise. Keep load conservative; avoid heavy weighted calf eccentrics or high-volume calf work.
- Focus: controlled movement, single-leg balance, and hip/glute control to offload the Achilles.
Easy aerobic (Thu) — treadmill
- 25–40 minutes steady easy (RPE 3–4). 0.5–1% incline to simulate outdoors. Relaxed cadence, steady breathing.
- If Achilles pain increases above ~3/10, stop and downgrade to brisk walk or bike/elliptical for the same duration.
Skills/form (Sat)
- Warm-up 10–15 min easy.
- Pickups: 6 × 20–30s controlled pickups (not all-out) with full recovery 2–3 min easy between efforts. If on treadmill, keep speeds gentle and short.
- Finish with ~10 min mobility/core and single-leg balance drills.
- If unsure or Achilles feels sketchy, replace pickups with form drills and balance work or a short easy cross-train.
Warm-up / cool-down and tendon check
- Do 5–8 min easy before each session, add dynamic mobility on strength day and light calf mobilization after.
- Check Achilles after the warm-up: if pain > ~3/10, replace the session with low-impact cross-training or mobility work.
Treadmill tips & safety
- Use 0.5–1% incline; avoid high-speed strides or long intervals. Keep pickups controlled and short. Monitor foot strike and cadence — shorten stride if the tendon feels tight.
Energy & pain rules
- If energy is low, shorten sessions to 15–20 min rather than forcing full sessions.
- If Achilles worsens, swap runs for bike/elliptical/walk; do not try to ‘make up’ missed work later in the week.
Quick question to finalize: which of Tue/Thu/Sat is your longest uninterrupted slot, and does the tiny gym have dumbbells/free weights or only bodyweight/bands? Confirm and I’ll lock exact minutes and conservative loads.

inbound.subject: Travel details
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 3. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate is yellow because I am traveling for work. Week is chaotic true. Equipment access is treadmill and gym, no track. I can do 40m treadmill runs and one short strength session.

outbound.subject: Re: Travel details
outbound.text:
Thanks — got your travel details. Good news: pain is low (2/10) and we can keep you moving without risking the build. Below is the conservative 3-session travel week I'll lock in once you confirm two quick details.
Quick recap: your goal is 2026-05-17 (11 weeks away). You’re in week 9 of the return-safely block, traveling, with treadmill + a tiny gym available and pain 2/10 — so we’ll preserve progress with low-cost, repeatable work and controlled load on the Achilles.
Planned weekly skeleton (3 sessions)
1) Short strength (25–35 min) — focus: glutes, core, single-leg balance, light posterior chain. Use light loads/bands; avoid heavy eccentric calf loading or heavy calf raises. Keep reps controlled.
2) Easy treadmill run (25–40 min) — RPE 3–4, 0.5–1% incline, relaxed cadence; steady continuous effort. No strides, pickups, hills, or hard repeats.
3) Skills/form + short easy run or cross-train (20–35 min) — easy running drills and technique work at low effort, or bike/elliptiser if the Achilles twinges.
Session structure examples (exact minutes locked after you confirm availability)
- Warm-up: 5–8 min easy (walk/jog or easy pedal) with dynamic mobility.
- Main block: steady easy minutes per session (see session durations above).
- Cooldown: 3–5 min easy + light mobility/stretch focused on hips and calves.
- Keep transitions smooth and monitor pain throughout; stop or downgrade if irritation appears.
Contingency rules (clear thresholds)
- If pain rises to >3/10 during or after a session: immediately downgrade that session to low-impact (bike/elliptical) OR shorten treadmill to 10–15 min easy. Do not make up missed intensity later in the week.
- If soreness persists across days, shift remaining sessions to cross-train and tell me so we can adjust next week.
Recovery checklist for the week
- Prioritize steady sleep and simple hydration/nutrition habits.
- Use icing or soft-tissue self-massage if the Achilles feels irritated after sessions.
- Avoid extra calf-loading between sessions (no heavy heel raises or long standing calf work).
Next steps — reply with three quick items
A) Which of Tue / Thu / Sat gives you the longest uninterrupted slot, and exactly how many minutes you have that day. 
B) What equipment does the tiny gym have: dumbbells/free weights OR only bodyweight and bands? 
C) Do you accept this conservative travel week as outlined (yes/no) so I can lock minute targets and exact strength loads.
Once you reply with A–C I’ll lock the exact minutes and specific strength loads for the week.

Nice work logging a solid 40-minute hotel treadmill run. HR average around 146 for 4.5 miles with no worsening symptoms and sleep was okay; frustration/mental fatigue from travel is real, but your body handled the load safely. Given the travel context and the mild pain 2/10, we will stay conservative but keep you moving to protect consistency toward your event goal.

If energy is very low, shorten sessions; if energy rebounds, progress cautiously.
I will lock minute targets and loads once we confirm your available slots.

- Longest available slot among Tue/Thu/Sat this week?
- Do you accept the conservative travel-week plan (three sessions: easy aerobic, strength, easy aerobic) with low-to-moderate effort and minimal Achilles load?

- We will track pain (aim <3/10), energy, and sleep daily.
- We will adjust immediately if energy or sleep drops; avoid adding volume.
- We will confirm slots and targets, then check in again before your next planned session.

inbound.subject: Back home and Saturday is open
inbound.body: Good news: for the next month Saturday is open again, so I have more flexibility for the long run. Week to week I still want to stay at four days, but now the long run doesn't have to be Sunday every time.

outbound.subject: Re: Back home and Saturday is open
outbound.text:
Great news on Saturday opening up—thanks for the flexibility. Here’s an updated four-day base plan that protects your Achilles, respects your energy signals, and keeps you moving toward the May event.
Here’s what changed and why
- You’ll stay at four days per week, with the long run shiftable to Saturday to match your schedule. This aligns with your goal to rebuild safely after the layoff while still building base endurance.
- The plan remains conservative (minimum effective dose) to protect your Achilles as you re-enter running. We’ll keep sessions easy, smooth, and technically tidy, with a clear emphasis on sleep, nutrition, and stress management.
- The current weekly skeleton remains simple and repeatable: four days with easy aerobic work, strength, and skills, plus a longer easy run on the open long-run day. Provisional session feel: easy aerobic on Day 1, strength on Day 2, skills on Day 3, and a long easy run on Day 4 when available. Exact minute targets will be locked once you confirm the longest uninterrupted slot you have (Tue/Thu/Sat) and you’re onboard with the 3-session travel-week structure.
- Safety rules you already signaled stay in place: if energy drops, cut the session short; if pain worsens, back off immediately; keep effort at relaxed, technique-focused levels. The aim is consistent, pain-free movement that progressively rebuilds resilience toward the half marathon.
Current block context
- You’re in week 11 of the return-to-training, with 10 weeks to the May 17 event. The focus remains: move safely, rebuild tolerance, and set up a sustainable base that supports the race plan.
What to confirm next
- Longest uninterrupted slot you have among Tue, Thu, or Sat, and whether you’re on board with the 3-session travel-week structure.
- The exact minutes for that long-run/Day 4 slot once you confirm your available window.
- Acceptance of the travel-week structure and the four-day format going forward.
Safety signals and decision rules
- If energy drops further, cut the session short rather than forcing completion.
- If you rebound tomorrow, resume with caution rather than making up missed work.
- If pain worsens, back off immediately and reassess technique, load, and recovery.
Check-ins and plan adjustments
- I’ll check in after the next few days to confirm you’re tolerating the load and to nudge minute targets if needed while keeping you on the safe path.
Additional resource
- For deeper context on progression and pain monitoring, I recommend reviewing the Achilles load management guide (good reference for safe progression during return to training).

inbound.subject: Family chaos week
inbound.body: This week got blown up by family stuff. I only got in one 30m run and I'm feeling low energy today. Can we just simplify everything for a few days and keep the important things important?

outbound.subject: Re: Family chaos week
outbound.text:
Totally fine — family comes first. Good work getting one 30‑minute run in. I hear you: keep things simple for a few days and protect what matters most.
You’re in week 11 of the return-to-training block; with 9 weeks until your goal on 2026-05-17, this week is about holding the line — protect the Achilles and conserve energy so we can keep building.
Simplified plan for the next few days (two anchors only):
- Anchor 1 — easy aerobic run: 30–40 minutes if energy allows; 20 minutes is OK if you feel low. Keep it conversational, RPE ~3–4.
- Anchor 2 — strength/mobility touch: one 10–20 minute session focused on single-leg strength, ankle/hip mobility, and calf control.
- Everything else: optional short mobility/recovery only (5–10 minutes) — no hard efforts, no strides, no tempo.
Intensity and pain rules
- Keep everything easy: conversational pace, RPE 3–4. Stay tall in posture and favor a slightly quicker cadence.
- If Achilles pain increases above ~3/10 during a session, stop or back off; if pain is worse the next morning, skip running and tell me.
- No hard sessions this week — I’ll keep the plan conservative while we protect your Achilles and energy.
Long run note
- If the long run needs to move, we can shift it to Saturday. Tell me how many uninterrupted minutes you have available Saturday, or if you’d prefer to skip/shorten it this weekend.
If things get tighter
- If your week becomes busier or energy drops, keep only the two anchors. Don’t try to make up missed work — consistency wins here.
Practical recovery reminders
- Prioritize sleep and simple hydration; choose low-friction, easy-to-eat foods rather than complicated meal prep.
- Use 5–10 minute mobility sessions on busier days to maintain movement without adding fatigue.
Why this helps (brief)
- Keeping load conservative now protects the Achilles, preserves consistency toward the race, and prevents pushing through low energy that would cost more time later.
Two quick replies I need to lock this week:
1) Are you available Saturday and how many uninterrupted minutes can you do for the long run (or do you prefer to skip/shorten)?
2) Do you accept the 3-session conservative travel/week structure so I can lock minute targets and strength loads?
Next step
- Once you confirm Saturday minutes and the travel-week acceptance (or report any pain/energy changes), I’ll update and lock the week. Check in briefly after you complete the two anchors so I can confirm next week’s progress.
Talk soon — I’ll hold this conservative plan until you reply.

inbound.subject: Details for the family-chaos week
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 2. Pain score: 3 out of 10. Risk candidate is yellow. Family stuff blew up this week. I only got in a 30m run. Felt low energy, sore, poor sleep. Stress score is 9. I am worried about losing momentum.

outbound.subject: Re: Details for the family-chaos week
outbound.text:
Thanks for the update — I’m glad you got one 30‑minute run in despite a rough week. I hear the stress and worry about losing momentum; that’s normal and fixable.
I’m sorry family stuff blew the week up — I see low energy, poor sleep, and a stress score of 9. Getting that 30‑minute run was the right small win; it keeps the thread of consistency alive.
Plan for this week (keep it very simple): two controlled easy aerobic sessions only. No hard efforts and no make‑up logic — repeatable completion is the win right now. You’re in week 12 of the return_safely block with 8 weeks until the 2026‑05‑17 event; this week’s goal is to protect the Achilles and rebuild reliable habits, not to progress load.
Sessions (concrete ranges)
- Session A (midweek): 20–35 minutes easy aerobic. Keep effort conversational; cadence light, posture tall.
- Session B (Saturday): 40–60 minutes easy aerobic. Please confirm the exact uninterrupted minutes you have available on Saturday (40 / 50 / 60) or if you prefer a shorter option (25–30 minutes).
Safety rules and pain thresholds (exact):
- Stop or sharply back off if Achilles pain goes above 3/10 during the session, or if it’s worse the next morning. Downgrade to walking or rest if pain increases.
- No hard or quality work until Achilles pain is consistently ≤1–2/10 and you have several symptom‑free days.
- If overall fatigue or stress rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to the lower end of the time range or skip.
Recovery priorities (low cost, high value this week):
- Sleep first: aim for an extra 30–60 minutes total night sleep if possible.
- After one run (optional): 8–12 minutes mobility/strength focused on single‑leg strength and calf control (slow, controlled reps).
- Hydration and short stress breaks (5‑10 minute walk or breathing practice during high‑stress parts of the day).
Why conservative now: you’ve got limited days and a yellow risk on the Achilles. Holding volume low and consistent protects momentum — each completed easy session is useful information and keeps you moving toward the event without provoking setbacks.
Next steps for me:
- Confirm which Saturday option you have (40 / 50 / 60 minutes) or if you want the shorter 25–30 min choice.
- Reply to confirm you accept this 2‑session simplified week.
- Check in after completing the two sessions and I’ll set targets for next week based on symptoms, sleep, and how those runs felt.
Reply with the Saturday minutes and a quick yes/no on the plan, and we’ll lock it in.
Coach

- Stop or sharply back off if Achilles pain goes above 3/10 during the session, or if it’s worse the next morning. Downgrade to walking or rest if pain increases.
- No hard or quality work until Achilles pain is consistently ≤1–2/10 and you have several symptom‑free days.
- If overall fatigue or stress rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to the lower end of the time range or skip.

Recovery priorities (low cost, high value this week):
- Sleep first: aim for an extra 30–60 minutes total night sleep if possible.
- After one run (optional): 8–12 minutes mobility/strength focused on single‑leg strength and calf control (slow, controlled reps).
- Hydration and short stress breaks (5‑10 minute walk or breathing practice during high‑stress parts of the day).

Why conservative now: you’ve got limited days and a yellow risk on the Achilles. Holding volume low and consistent protects momentum — each completed easy session is useful information and keeps you moving toward the event without provoking setbacks.

Next steps for me:
- Confirm which Saturday option you have (40 / 50 / 60 minutes) or if you want the shorter 25–30 min choice.
- Reply to confirm you accept this 2‑session simplified week.
- Check in after completing the two sessions and I’ll set targets for next week based on symptoms, sleep, and how those runs felt.

Reply with the Saturday minutes and a quick yes/no on the plan, and we’ll lock it in.

Coach

inbound.subject: Back on track
inbound.body: Better week. I ran three times: 35m easy, 50m steady, and 80m today for about 9 miles. Felt good, slept well, and the Achilles feels quiet again. Same four-day rhythm still seems like the sweet spot.

outbound.subject: Re: Back on track
outbound.text:
Nice work getting back on track and noting the Achilles feels quiet again.
You're in Week 13 of the return-to-training block, eight weeks out from your goal event on 2026-05-17. The current plan remains base-building with a four-day rhythm: easy loads, steady effort, and a longer session, with Friday kept as an easy/maintenance day if needed.
Better week this week. Your three runs totaled about 9 miles – 35 minutes easy, 50 minutes steady, and 80 minutes today. Energy is up, sleep was solid, and the Achilles remains quiet. We’ll stay conservative and aligned with safe base-building, keeping loads easy and the four-day rhythm intact.
Safety priorities stay at the top: sleep, nutrition, and stress management. If energy drops, shorten the session rather than forcing completion; if you rebound, resume cautiously rather than trying to "make up" work.
Please confirm the exact uninterrupted Saturday long-run minutes you can commit to this week so I can lock in the plan for continuity.
For this week, the plan remains easy_aerobic, tempo, and strength in that order. The next session will be easy (easy_aerobic). We’ll monitor energy and response and progress with a conservative progression unless energy remains strong.

inbound.subject: Should I add strides?
inbound.body: Question for you: since things are stable again, should I add strides after one easy run, or keep everything fully aerobic for now?

outbound.subject: Re: Should I add strides?
outbound.text:
Good question — nice restraint. Since this is the first stable week after your Achilles niggle, I recommend staying conservative.
You’re in week 13 of the return-safely block with 7 weeks until your event (2026-05-17); this week should prioritize stable, pain-free miles.
Short recommendation: Keep all runs fully aerobic for now — no strides this week.
Why: strides are an intensity exposure. After a recent yellow flag the safest path is protecting consistency and avoiding anything that could re-trigger the Achilles.
Concrete gate to reintroduce (must meet all):
- At least 7–10 consecutive training days with no symptom worsening (during the session or the next morning).
- Ability to complete easy runs without guarding or altered mechanics.
- A clean long-run response (no increased pain or stiffness the next day).
If you want an earlier, very low-risk test (only after you meet the gate): one single session on a separate day — 4–6 × 10–20s controlled pickups on flat ground with full walk/jog recovery. Stop immediately for any twinge and report the next-morning response.
How I’ll judge progress: we’ll reintroduce only one intensity element at first, watch the 24–48 hour response closely, then repeat the exposure before increasing duration or frequency.
Check in after your next long run — tell me how it felt during the run and the next morning — and I’ll green-light the next step.
—Coach

inbound.subject: Harder session left me cooked
inbound.body: Yesterday I did a harder session: total run 55m with 6 x 3 minutes moderate-hard. Today I'm pretty cooked, sleep was poor, and soreness is definitely up. I don't think anything is injured, but it feels like I pushed the edge.

outbound.subject: Re: Harder session left me cooked
outbound.text:
Thanks for the update. It’s a solid sign you’re tuning in to your body after that tougher session, especially with the sleep hit and soreness.
You’re in Week 14 of the return-safely block, six weeks out from your goal event on 2026-05-17. Based on how you feel, we’re keeping the plan simple this week to protect your recovery and Achilles niggle. No strides or hard efforts. Anchor two easy aerobic sessions and the short mobility/recovery touch, with the long run on Saturday kept flexible. Friday is an easy day. If you feel more wiped, lean into even more rest and rely on sleep, hydration, and gentle movement. We’ll re-evaluate strides only after you’ve shown consistent symptom stability for a full week and a smooth long run response.
What to anchor this week
- Two easy aerobic sessions (anchor 1 and anchor 2)

inbound.subject: Numbers from the hard session
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 5 out of 10. Risk candidate feels yellow. Intervals yesterday: run 55m total with 6 x 3 minutes moderate-hard. Felt exhausted after, soreness high, sleep poor. Pain affects form false. Pain worsening false. No swelling.

outbound.subject: Re: Numbers from the hard session
outbound.text:
Thanks — good detail and the numbers help. You handled a tough session yesterday; your follow-up report flags real fatigue, so I want to keep this week deliberately conservative.
You said: 55 min total yesterday with 6x3' moderate-hard intervals, exhaustion, high soreness, poor sleep, pain 5/10 but not worsening and no swelling. That’s a clear yellow-risk signal — we pull back now to protect consistency and form.
Today (minimum effective dose)
- 20–30 minutes very easy aerobic (walking plus easy jog allowed). Keep it conversational/easy. Stop early if energy or pain worsens.
Rest of this week (you have 4 available days)
- Two additional easy aerobic runs: 30–40 min each, easy effort.
- One light strength/mobility session: 20–30 min, very low load (bodyweight, bands, gentle core and hip work, mobility and foam roll).
- Short, flexible long run on Saturday: recommend 25 or 30 minutes this week (your call — confirm below).
- Total weekly volume: reduce by ~20% versus a usual week.
Hard-effort rules (non-negotiable this week)
- No intervals, no tempos, no strides, no testing. Do not try to "make up" yesterday’s session.
- If energy drops or pain worsens during any session, cut it short rather than pushing through.
Recovery actions to prioritize
- Sleep target: aim for consistent bedtime and wake time, 7–9 hours if possible. Try a 30–45 minute wind-down (low-light, no screens) and stop caffeine ~6 hours before bed.
- Eat a bit more carbs + protein after sessions (simple: yogurt/banana + sandwich, recovery drink, etc.).
- Hydrate, use short gentle mobility/foam-roll sessions, and keep active recovery walks.
What I need from you
- Confirm the Saturday long-run target: 25 or 30 minutes.
- Check in after your next run with how it felt during and the next morning (energy, soreness, pain). Reassess after that morning report; we will only consider reintroducing intensity after multiple clean days.
- Report any worsening of pain, new swelling, or any other red flags immediately.
Why we’re conservative now and the timeline
- You’re in week 15 of the return-to-training block with 6 weeks until your event on 2026-05-17. Yesterday was a hard stimulus on a fragile baseline (poor sleep, soreness, niggle). Pulling volume and removing intensity now keeps you consistent and lowers injury risk.
- Gating for intensity: we need 7–10 consecutive symptom-free training days and a clean long-run response before bringing back hard efforts.
Short reassurance and next step
- This short, conservative week protects the larger goal. Confirm Saturday (25 or 30 min) and check in after your next run + next-morning report — I’ll update the plan based on that.

inbound.subject: Three-day week details
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 3. Pain score: 3 out of 10. Risk candidate is yellow. Next week I only have three training days. Week is chaotic false, but volume has to come down. Please simplify the week and protect the key session.

outbound.subject: Re: Three-day week details
outbound.text:
Thanks — got it. I see the event date and that you only have three training days next week, with pain about 3/10. Good call to simplify and protect one key session.
Recap: Event 2026-05-17; 4 weeks out; you have 3 training days next week; baseline pain ~3/10 (yellow risk). Goal: keep volume low, no intensity, and protect the one longer aerobic session.
Three-day plan (pick which calendar days work — you can shift them):
- Day A — Short easy aerobic: 25–30 minutes easy run (conversational).
- Day B — Short strength + mobility (non-impact): 20–25 minutes total. Brief easy 10–15 minute jog or brisk walk + 10 minutes targeted strength/mobility (see below).
- Day C — Key protected longer easy aerobic: 40–55 minutes easy run (the week’s priority). Keep it purely easy.
Execution guidance
- Pace/effort: conversational RPE ~3/10. If you’re having to talk in short sentences, back off.
- Warm-up/cooldown: before runs, 5–8 min very easy jog or brisk walk; after, 5–8 min easy jog/walk and light hip/glute mobility.
- Strength/mobility (Day B): 2 rounds of 6–10 slow single-leg glute bridges, 8–12 banded lateral walks, 30–45s plank or dead-bug, plus 5 minutes hip mobility. Keep loads light and controlled; stop any exercise that sharpens or increases pain.
- If time is tighter: shorten each session by ~25% rather than increasing intensity. If you must drop a session, drop a non-key (do not move intensity into another day).
Safety rules (concrete)
- If pain during a session rises to 5/10 or higher, stop immediately. Do not continue through that pain.
- If pain increases by 2 or more points from baseline (e.g., 3 → 5) at any time, stop the session and rest.
- If next-morning pain is worse than baseline by 1+ point or is >3/10, skip the key session and treat the week as conservative — do not add intensity or make up the missed work.
- If you notice new swelling, numbness, tingling, or sharp localized pain, stop training and contact me.
- Explicit: do not add intervals, tempo, strides, or any planned intensity this week. Do not try to make up missed sessions later in the week.
What I need back after the week
- Which sessions you completed and any schedule shifts.
- Pain score during each session and the next-morning pain score.
- Perceived effort (easy/comfortable or slightly harder) and sleep quality that week.
- Any unexpected issues (swelling, sharp pain, numbness).
Why this helps and next steps
- A conservative, protected week reduces risk of aggravation while keeping you consistent 4 weeks from the race. If symptoms are stable or improved, we’ll either repeat one conservative week or progress one training lever (duration first) — only if pain stays controlled. If symptoms worsen, we’ll maintain protection and re-evaluate.
Keep it simple and responsive. Send the session outcomes and pain scores after each run and again the next morning so I can decide the right next move.

inbound.subject: Race confidence check
inbound.body: With the race getting closer, do you think the current plan still fits where I am? I'm curious what the biggest focus should be over the next two weeks, especially since I've settled into that four-day pattern pretty well.

outbound.subject: Re: Race confidence check
outbound.text:
Great question — and good to hear you’ve found a four-day rhythm.
Short answer: yes, the current plan still fits, but for the next two weeks we stay conservative given your baseline Achilles pain (~3/10) and recent “yellow” history. Rationale: keeping intensity out for now limits load spikes that provoke flare-ups while preserving easy aerobic work.
Biggest focus for the next 2 weeks:
- Keep consistent, low-cost running and avoid spikes in volume or intensity.
- No hard efforts: hold off on intervals, tempos, strides, or fast finishes.
- Protect the Achilles with daily rehab and targeted strength/mobility.
- Monitor closely: stop a session if pain rises to ≥5/10 or increases by 2+ points from baseline; report during-session and next-morning pain for key sessions; shorten rather than increase load; do not try to compensate for missed intensity later.
Practical 4-day weekly skeleton (examples):
- Day 1: short easy run 25–35 min.
- Day 2: strength + mobility session 20–30 min; optional brief easy jog/walk.
- Day 3: easy run 30–45 min (or cross-train if pain spikes).
- Day 4: protected long easy aerobic session — progress toward your target time; keep effort conversational.
Note: keep all runs easy; no intervals, tempos, strides, or fast finishes for now.
Specific Achilles actions:
- Alfredson-style eccentric calf protocol 1–2x/day.
- 10–15 min targeted calf + hip/glute strength and mobility daily.
- Use icing or compression after longer runs as needed.
Monitoring and safety rules (restated):
- Stop the session if pain reaches ≥5/10 or increases by 2+ points from your baseline.
- Report during-session and next-morning pain for each key session (particularly the protected long).
- When in doubt, shorten or skip; we won’t add intensity now.
Progression criteria (when we’ll consider reintroducing any speed):
- At least 7–10 consecutive days of training with no increase in pain and no next-morning flare, plus consistent completion of the protected long run.
- If those are met, we’ll add only one very controlled stimulus to test tolerance — most likely 4–6 controlled strides — then reassess before any further change.
I recommend a short Alfredson protocol demo video on YouTube for the eccentric exercises; tell me if you want the link and I’ll send it.
Quick asks for you:
- Which day do you prefer for the protected long run over the next two weeks?
- Do you want the plan written explicitly for four days?
- How did today’s/this week’s Achilles pain behave — during runs and the next morning?
You’re in week 17 of the return-safely block with three weeks until the event on 2026-05-17; these two weeks are about protecting progress, not adding stress. Tell me the long-run day and today’s pain report and I’ll send the four-day plan.

inbound.subject: Mild cold and weather
inbound.body: I have a mild cold and the weather is awful this week. Energy is not great. I don't want to do anything dumb, but I also don't want to lose the thread completely. How conservative should I be?

outbound.subject: Re: Mild cold and weather
outbound.text:
Thanks — good call flagging this. Mild colds + low energy + bad weather are exactly when a conservative approach protects the training thread without risking a bigger setback.
You’re in week 18 of the return-to-training block, three weeks out from your half on 2026-05-17. This week’s job is staying healthy and preserving the aerobic base — not chasing hard sessions. Follow the guidelines below for the next 48 hours and the short term.
Immediate (today → next 48 h)
- If you have fever, chest symptoms, dizziness or unusual breathlessness: stop training, rest, and message me right away.
- If symptoms are mild (congestion, sore throat) and energy is low: choose rest or 20–40 minutes very easy bike/elliptical/walk.
- If you do run: keep it ≤30 minutes, very easy and flat. No intensity, no strides/hills. Stop if it feels worse.
Short-term training rules
- No intervals, tempos, or strides for at least 7 days after symptoms fully clear.
- While symptoms persist or energy is low, reduce planned volume by ~30–50%.
- Don’t use a single “good” day as clearance to jump back into normal workload — progress one thing at a time.
Weather & modality options
- If outdoor running is unsafe this week, use a treadmill or low-impact cardio (bike/elliptical/swim) to preserve aerobic stimulus.
- If Achilles twinges increase, favour non‑impact options until pain returns to baseline.
Achilles rehab (daily)
- Keep doing Alfredson eccentrics plus 10–15 minutes of calf/hip strength and mobility each day.
- If Achilles pain increases by ≥2/10 or to ≥5/10 during/after sessions, stop running and tell me.
Decision thresholds & timeline
- Require 24–48 hours of clear symptom improvement before resuming normal easy runs.
- Require ~7 consecutive days of easy, pain‑free training before reintroducing any intensity.
- Contact me immediately for fever, chest symptoms, or if next‑morning symptoms worsen.
What I need from you
- Tell me if you have fever or chest symptoms now.
- Tell me how your energy feels tomorrow morning.
- Tell me whether you’d like to shift the protected long run — Saturday is available this week.
Do not try to make up missed intensity while still symptomatic or low on energy, and don’t push through fever, chest symptoms, dizziness, or unusual shortness of breath. Keep the rehab work non‑negotiable — it’s low cost and high return.
Tell me the three items above tomorrow morning and I’ll adjust the plan for the week accordingly.
